questions.question: What does the bitwise AND operation (&) do in the expression (x - 1) & x?

options.A: Shifts the bits of x to the left and fills the leftmost bits with the value 1
options.B: Picks the least significant 1-bit in the binary representation of x and clears all the lower-order bits
options.C: Performs a bitwise XOR operation between x and 1
options.D: Picks the most significant 1-bit in the binary representation of x and sets all the lower-order bits to 1

questions.explanation: The expression (x - 1) & x is a common pattern for checking if a bit is set in x. The expression effectively clears all the lower-order bits of x, leaving only the least significant 1-bit. The bitwise AND operation (&) then picks this bit and sets all the lower-order bits to 0.

questions.question: How do you check if a number is a power of two?
questions.explanation: A number is a power of two if and only if it has exactly one 1-bit in its binary representation. The bitwise AND operation (&) between a number and the number minus one will result in zero if and only if the number is a power of two.

questions.question: Why does the expression `x & (-x)` result in a number with only one 1-bit?

options.A: Because the bitwise NOT operation (~) inverts all the bits of x
options.B: Because the bitwise AND operation (&) picks the least significant 1-bit in the binary representation of x
options.C: Because the bitwise NOT operation (~) and bitwise AND operation (&) together have a complementary effect on the bits of x
options.D: Because the expression results in a number with no bits set to 1

questions.explanation: The bitwise NOT operation (~) inverts all the bits of x, and the expression `-x` computes the two's complement of x. When we perform a bitwise AND operation (&) between x and -x, all the bits that were not set to 1 in either x and -x will be cleared, leaving a number with only one 1-bit.

questions.question: What is the result of the expression `x & ((x - 1) | y)`?

options.C: The least significant non-zero bits of x or y
options.D: The most significant non-zero bits of x or y

questions.explanation: The expression `x & ((x - 1) | y)` first computes the bitwise OR operation between `(x - 1)` and `y`, which picks the least significant non-zero bits of `y` that are also set in `x`. The bitwise AND operation between `x` and the result then picks the least significant non-zero bits of `x` that are also set in the result.

questions.question: Can you use the power property of bitwise operations to simplify the expression `x == 1 || x & (x - 1) == 0`?

options.A: Yes, using bitwise AND with the complement of the input
options.B: Yes, by using a recursive approach
options.C: No, it is not possible to simplify the expression
options.D: Yes, using the fact that `x` is a power of two

questions.explanation: The expression can be simplified to `x == 1 || (x & (x - 1))`, which is equivalent to `x == 1 || x & ~x == x == 1`. We can then rewrite the expression to a single condition `x & ~x == 0` using the fact that an XOR with the complement has a complementary effect, i.e., `x == (~x ^ y).

questions.question: Can you implement the function `isPowerOfTwo` in C with the help of bitwise operations?
questions.explanation: The function can be implemented using the property that a number is a power of two if and only if it has exactly one 1-bit in its binary representation. The expression `(x & (x - 1)) == 0` is equivalent to checking if `x` is a power of two. The `&&` operator has higher precedence than the `==` operator, so the expression is equivalent to `x == 1 || (x & (x - 1) == 0)`. This function can be used efficiently, since it only requires a constant number of memory accesses and no loops or recursive calls.
